title: Ransomware
description: Malware that encrypts victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key.
fullExplanation: Ransomware encrypts files on infected systems and demands cryptocurrency payment for the decryption key. Modern ransomware uses asymmetric encryption (RSA) to encrypt a symmetric key (AES), making decryption impossible without the attacker's private key. Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) allows non-technical criminals to deploy ransomware. Double extortion adds data theft — pay or we publish your data.

notableExamples.name: WannaCry (2017)
notableExamples.description: Used EternalBlue SMB exploit — infected 200,000+ systems in 150 countries in 3 days

notableExamples.name: NotPetya (2017)
notableExamples.description: Disguised as ransomware, actually a wiper — $10B in damages, most destructive cyberattack ever

notableExamples.name: REvil / Sodinokibi
notableExamples.description: RaaS group — Kaseya attack (2021) hit 1,500 businesses via MSP supply chain

notableExamples.name: LockBit
notableExamples.description: Most prolific RaaS 2022-2024 — automated, fast encryption, double extortion

notableExamples.name: Colonial Pipeline (2021)
notableExamples.description: DarkSide ransomware — shut down US East Coast fuel supply for 5 days

attackChain.description: Phishing email, RDP brute force, VPN vulnerability, supply chain
Install backdoor, create admin accounts, disable security tools
Spread across network using stolen credentials, PsExec, WMI
Steal sensitive data for double extortion leverage
Deploy ransomware payload — encrypt files with AES+RSA
Display ransom demand with payment instructions (Bitcoin/Monero)

keyFacts: RaaS: developers take 20-30%, affiliates keep 70-80% of ransom
Offline backups are the most effective defense
Paying ransom doesn't guarantee decryption — 20% never recover data
Double extortion: encrypt + threaten to publish stolen data
Triple extortion adds DDoS attack on victim's infrastructure

defenses.name: Offline Backups (3-2-1)
defenses.description: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite — test restores regularly

defenses.name: EDR / Antivirus
defenses.description: Behavioral detection stops encryption before it completes

defenses.name: Network Segmentation
defenses.description: Limit lateral movement — ransomware can't reach all systems

defenses.name: Patch Management
defenses.description: EternalBlue, Log4Shell — patch critical vulns immediately

defenses.name: Disable RDP / MFA on RDP
defenses.description: RDP is #1 ransomware entry point — restrict or require MFA

defenses.name: Least Privilege
defenses.description: Limit user permissions — ransomware can only encrypt accessible files
